title: Jesus' Baptism
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry
source_files: Jesus’ Baptism - Matthew 3_13-17 Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Why Is Jesus Being Baptized? The Problem John Saw
subpoints: John's baptism was a baptism of repentance — Jesus had nothing to repent of, yet he came to be baptized
John tried to prevent him (v. 14): I need to be baptized by you — why are you coming to me?
Jesus answers: it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness (v. 15) — his baptism is not about his own sin but about fulfilling a divine necessity
The word 'then' in verse 13 connects Jesus's arrival to John's preaching — Jesus comes at the exact moment John is baptizing and preaching repentance

outline.title: Fulfilling All Righteousness: What It Means
subpoints: Jesus fulfills righteousness not by confessing sin but by identifying with the sinners he came to save — taking their place
The baptism is the kickoff event for Jesus's public ministry: for the first time he is doing something public to disclose who he is and what his mission is
John 1:29 — 'Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' — is John's public declaration that Jesus is the Messiah, prompted by what happened at the baptism
Jesus identifies with the nation of Israel: as Israel passed through the water at the Red Sea, Jesus passes through the water of the Jordan at the beginning of his representative mission

outline.title: The Trinitarian Confirmation: What the Heavens Declared
subpoints: At the baptism all three persons of the Trinity are visibly present: the Son being baptized, the Spirit descending like a dove, and the Father speaking from heaven
The opened heavens echo Genesis 1:2 (the Spirit hovering over the waters) and anticipate the new creation that Christ has come to inaugurate
The voice from heaven declares: 'This is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased' — combining Psalm 2:7 (the royal Son) with Isaiah 42:1 (the suffering Servant)
God is well pleased with Jesus before his public ministry has properly begun — the pleasure is grounded in who he is, not primarily what he has yet done

outline.title: The Implications for Believers
subpoints: Jesus is the suffering servant who stands in the place of Israel and God's people before God, freed from their sins and from the wrath to come
Because God is well pleased with Jesus, God is well pleased with all who are in Christ — not because of their merits, but because of Christ's

Christian baptism connects to Jesus's baptism: as Jesus went through water in identification with sinners, believers go through water in identification with Christ's death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4)
Hold on to the suffering servant who is also the Son of God — he suffered on the cross and rose from the dead for our sake

summary:
Preaching in the afternoon service from Matthew 3:13-17, Pastor Tito Lyro examines the baptism of Jesus — the kickoff event for his public ministry. The sermon begins with the question that immediately strikes most readers: why is Jesus, who has no sin, being baptized by John whose baptism is specifically a baptism of repentance? John himself raises this objection in verse 14. Jesus's answer — 'it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness' — establishes that his baptism is not about his own sin but about his representative identification with the sinners he came to save, and about fulfilling the divine purpose for his mission.

The baptism is understood as the public kickoff of Jesus's ministry: before this moment, Jesus has done nothing publicly to identify himself or his mission. At the baptism, the heavens are opened and all three persons of the Trinity are visibly present: the Son being baptized, the Spirit descending like a dove (echoing Genesis 1:2 and the new creation), and the Father speaking from heaven. The Father's words — 'This is my beloved Son in whom I am well pleased' — combine Psalm 2:7 (the royal Messianic Son) with Isaiah 42:1 (the suffering Servant of the Lord), marking Jesus as both King and Servant in a single declaration.

The sermon closes with the gospel application: because the Father is well pleased with Jesus, he is well pleased with all who are in Christ — not because of their own merit, but because of Christ's. The suffering Servant stood in the place of his people, bore the wrath to come, and rose from the dead for their sake. Christian baptism is connected to Jesus's baptism: as he went through water in identification with sinners, believers go through water in identification with his death and resurrection.
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 3:13-17

For our Scripture reading this afternoon, we are turning to the Gospel of Matthew chapter 3, starting at verse 13 and reading through verse 17. So Matthew 3, verse 13. This is the word of our Lord.

*Then Jesus came from Galilee to John at the Jordan to be baptized by him. And John tried to prevent Him, saying, "I need to be baptized by You, and are You coming to me?" But Jesus answered and said to him, "Permit it to be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness." Then he allowed Him. When He had been baptized, Jesus came up immediately from the water; and behold, the heavens were opened to Him, and He saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting upon Him. And suddenly a voice came from heaven, saying, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased."*

This is the word of our Lord. Let's pray together. Father, thank you for Your word. We pray that You speak to us even now. We pray that You give me clarity of mind and clarity of words, and that Your Spirit would grab the words and apply them to our hearts. We pray in Jesus' name. Amen.

## Introduction: John's Objection

When we read this passage, most of us react as John the baptizer reacted in verse 14. John says: I need to be baptized by You, and You are coming to me? My baptism is a baptism of repentance. I need to be baptized by You. That's John's argument — and it's an argument that makes sense. Why is Jesus being baptized by the servant? Why is the Fulfiller being baptized by the Preparer?

Yet it was important — it was necessary — for Jesus's ministry that he be baptized by the one who came to prepare the way. In fact, he puts it in terms of necessity: it is fitting — it is necessary — to fulfill all righteousness.

## When Is This Happening?

So in verse 13, Matthew says: *Then Jesus came from Galilee to John at the Jordan to be baptized by him.* That first word, "then," refers to the time that John was baptizing and teaching the crowds, as described in the previous verses. As John is baptizing, as John is telling them to repent, as John is preaching repentance and the kingdom of heaven is at hand — at that exact moment Jesus comes to be baptized by him.

That's why Jesus's baptism is considered the beginning of his public ministry, even though this first year of his ministry is kind of quiet as far as public notoriety goes. For the first time, Jesus is doing something in public to disclose who he is and what his mission is. This is — in modern terms — the kickoff event for the public ministry of Jesus Christ.

We're going to see more of this in a moment: but it is at this moment that John says — in John 1:29 — "Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world" — a public declaration that Jesus is the Messiah. This is what John had been sent to prepare people for.

## Fulfilling All Righteousness

In verse 14 John tried to prevent him, saying: *I need to be baptized by You, and are You coming to me?* But Jesus answered and said to him: *Permit it to be so now, for thus it is fitting for us to fulfill all righteousness. Then he allowed him.*

Now John the baptizer somehow realized that Jesus did not need to repent — he had no sin to confess. So why is Jesus being baptized?

Jesus says it is fitting — it is necessary — for us to fulfill all righteousness. Notice that he says "for us" — this is not just about Jesus alone but about the mission they share. John has been sent to prepare; Jesus has come to fulfill. And part of that fulfillment involves Jesus identifying with the people he has come to save.

Think of it this way. Jesus didn't just die on the cross as a stranger substituting for people he had nothing to do with. Throughout his ministry, Jesus identified himself with the sinners he came to save — eating with tax collectors and sinners, touching lepers, speaking to Samaritans. His baptism is the formal opening of that identification. He who had no sin enters the waters of John's baptism of repentance — taking his place alongside the sinners he came to represent.

Isaiah 53 describes the suffering servant: *He was numbered with the transgressors, and He bore the sin of many.* The baptism is the beginning of that numbering — the beginning of Jesus standing in the place of his people.

There is also a broader background. Israel passed through the waters of the Red Sea when God delivered them from Egypt. And now Jesus — who will be identified throughout Matthew's Gospel as the one who recapitulates the history of Israel, fulfilling what Israel failed to do — passes through the waters of the Jordan. It is a new Exodus being inaugurated. Jesus is the true Israel who will fulfill all righteousness that Israel failed to fulfill.

## The Heavens Open: The Trinitarian Declaration

Look at verses 16 and 17: *When He had been baptized, Jesus came up immediately from the water; and behold, the heavens were opened to Him, and He saw the Spirit of God descending like a dove and alighting upon Him. And suddenly a voice came from heaven, saying, "This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased."*

At the baptism, all three persons of the Trinity are visibly present. The Son is being baptized. The Spirit descends like a dove and alights on him. And the Father speaks from heaven. This is one of the most explicit Trinitarian moments in all of Scripture — the three persons present together, each acting in their distinctive way.

The dove — the Spirit descending like a dove — echoes Genesis 1:2, where the Spirit of God was hovering over the face of the waters at creation. Here the Spirit hovers over the waters of the Jordan as the new creation begins. Jesus has come to inaugurate a new creation — to undo what sin has done and to create a new people, a new world, by his death and resurrection. The Spirit's presence at the baptism signals that this mission has begun.

And the Father speaks from heaven: *This is My beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased.*

These words combine two great Old Testament texts. The phrase "My beloved Son" echoes Psalm 2:7: *You are My Son; today I have begotten You.* Psalm 2 is the great Messianic Psalm about the royal Son of God who will reign over the nations. The Father is declaring that Jesus is the long-awaited Messianic King.

But the phrase "in whom I am well pleased" echoes Isaiah 42:1: *Behold! My Servant whom I uphold, My Elect One in whom My soul delights! I have put My Spirit upon Him; He will bring forth justice to the Gentiles.* This is the first of the great Servant Songs in Isaiah — describing not a triumphant king but a humble, suffering servant who will be bruised and crushed for the sins of his people.

By combining Psalm 2 and Isaiah 42 in a single declaration, the Father is saying: Jesus is both. He is the royal Son and the suffering Servant. He will reign — but he will reign through suffering. He will conquer — but he will conquer by being crushed.

And notice: the Father says he is well pleased before Jesus has done anything in his public ministry. Before the miracles. Before the preaching. Before the cross. God is well pleased with Jesus at the beginning of his mission. Why? Because the Father's pleasure is not based on Jesus's performance — it is based on who Jesus is. He is the beloved Son, eternally loved by the Father, eternally pleasing to the Father.

## The Implications for Us

Now, what does all of this mean for us?

Jesus is the suffering servant who stands in the place of Israel and of God's people in the presence of God — freed from their sins and from the wrath to come that John had warned about just a moment before in his preaching.

Hold on to this suffering servant. Hold on to the Son of God. Hold on to the one who suffered on the cross for our honor and behalf, but also rose from the dead for our sake.

And here is the most wonderful thing: because God is well pleased with Jesus, God is well pleased with all who are in Christ. Not because of any of your merits — not because you have been good enough, or faithful enough, or religious enough. But because Christ Jesus stands before the Father, interceding for you, and the Father is well pleased with him. And in him, he is well pleased with you.

That is great hope. That is enormous comfort. Whatever you are going through, whatever failures you are carrying, whatever accusations your conscience or the enemy makes against you — if you are in Christ, the Father says: this is my beloved Son, in whom I am well pleased. And you are in him.

Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you that you are a God who saves, and that you saved us through Jesus Christ. Thank you that he stands before you interceding for us, and that you are well pleased with him. Thank you that you are also well pleased with us — not because of any of our merits, but because of Christ Jesus who stands before you. We thank you that you have opened our eyes to see that and to be joyful in the glory of the gospel. We pray these things in Jesus' name. Amen.
